coredata.dc:title: Crosslinked chitosan embedded TiO2 NPs and carbon dots-based nanocomposite: An excellent photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Midya, Lipi
Sarkar, Abanindra Nath
Das, Raghunath
Maity, Arjun
Pal, Sagar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a new hybrid nanocomposite, comprising of titania nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) and carbon dots (CDs) deposited polyvinyl imidazole crosslinked chitosan [cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs] has been developed. The nanocomposite has been synthesised by in-situ deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto the surface of the copolymer under microwave irradiation. To the best of our knowledge, this in-situ approach has effectively been applied for the first time to fabricate green fluorescent CDs from sugar cane juice at moderate temperature (75 °C) under microwave irradiation. The developed nanocomposite has been characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy, 13C NMR, XRD, HR-TEM, STEM and XPS analyses. The results suggest that the successful deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto the surface of crosslinked chitosan is achieved. The experimental studies indicate that the NPs/CDs-impregnated nanocomposite allows efficient photocatalytic degradation of toxic organic compounds (~98.6% degradation of 2,4-dicholorophenol, ~95.8% degradation of Reactive Blue 4, ~98.2% degradation of Reactive Red 15) in the presence of sunlight. Finally, LC-MS analysis of the resultant degraded materials reveals the formation of organic molecules with lower molecular mass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Microwave
Nanocomposite
Photocatalyst

No: DST/TM/WTI/2 K16/42 (G) ] and Council of Scientific & Industrial Research , New Delhi [Scheme No: 01(2892)/17/EMR-II ]. Authors earnestly acknowledge SAIF, IISc, Bangalore for providing solid state 13 C NMR data and CRF, IIT Kharagpur for LCMS data.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital image of cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs-CDs composite at 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (b) UV–vis diffusion reflectance spectra of cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs, cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs-CDs composite (Inset: band gap of cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs and cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs-CDs composite using Tauc's plot).

(a) The XRD pattern of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs composite, (b) HR-TEM image of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite, (c) lattice fringe of embedded CDs, and (d) lattice fringe of incorporated TiO2 NPs.
The STEM images of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs indicating C atom, O atom, Ti atom and presence of all atoms (C, O, Ti) in the analysed portion of the composite.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) Ti 2p.

Comparison of photocatalytic performance of (a) RB4 and (b) RR15 under sunlight exposure in the presence of different photocatalysts and the digital images of the corresponding degraded dye solutions: (1) dye solution before degradation, (2) dye solution after degradation in absence of photocatalyst, (3) dye solution after degradation in presence of cl-Ch-p(VI), (4) dye solution after degradation in presence of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs, (5) dye solution after degradation in presence of cl-Ch-p(VI)/CDs, and (6) dye solution after degradation in presence of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs.
Degradation of 2, 4-DCP using cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite with various time intervals.
(a) LC-MS spectrum of RB4 dye degradation using cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2-CDs composite. (b) Probable degradation fragments of RB4.
(a) LC-MS spectrum of RR15 dye degradation using cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2-CDs composite. (b) Probable degradation fragments of RR15.
(a) LC-MS spectrum of 2,4-DCP dye degradation using cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2-CDs composite. (b) Probable degradation fragments of 2,4-DCP.
Probable mechanism for the synthesis of cl-Ch-pVI (reused from Ref. [31]) and cl-Ch-pVI/TiO2NPs-CDs composite hydrogel.
Degradation mechanism of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs under sunlight irradiation.
Rate constant for RB4 and RR15 degradation under sunlight irradiation for 30 min.

head.ce:title: Crosslinked chitosan embedded TiO2 NPs and carbon dots-based nanocomposite: An excellent photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, Jharkhand, India-826004

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Science, University of Johannesburg, Johannesburg, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Science, University of Johannesburg, Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: DST/CSIR, Centre for Nanostructure and Advanced Materials (CeNAM), Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Pretoria 0001, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Nanostructure and Advanced Materials (CeNAM)
Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: DST/CSIR, Centre for Nanostructure and Advanced Materials (CeNAM), Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Pretoria 0001, South Africa

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Sagar Pal, Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM) Dhanbad, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a new hybrid nanocomposite, comprising of titania nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) and carbon dots (CDs) deposited polyvinyl imidazole crosslinked chitosan [cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs] has been developed. The nanocomposite has been synthesised by in-situ deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto the surface of the copolymer under microwave irradiation. To the best of our knowledge, this in-situ approach has effectively been applied for the first time to fabricate green fluorescent CDs from sugar cane juice at moderate temperature (75 °C) under microwave irradiation. The developed nanocomposite has been characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy, 13C NMR, XRD, HR-TEM, STEM and XPS analyses. The results suggest that the successful deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto the surface of crosslinked chitosan is achieved. The experimental studies indicate that the NPs/CDs-impregnated nanocomposite allows efficient photocatalytic degradation of toxic organic compounds (~98.6% degradation of 2,4-dicholorophenol, ~95.8% degradation of Reactive Blue 4, ~98.2% degradation of Reactive Red 15) in the presence of sunlight. Finally, LC-MS analysis of the resultant degraded materials reveals the formation of organic molecules with lower molecular mass.

ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of toxic organic compounds using promising photocatalyst is an effective green technique for the treatment of wastewater. In particular, polymer nanocomposites are considered as unique multifunctional photocatalysts owing to the combined activities of both the polymers and nanomaterials [1,2]. Furthermore, polymeric materials show the vast architectural diversity and multifunctional surface chemistry along with good mechanical strength, which facilitate for long-term applications under various conditions. Several literature reports demonstrate the incorporation of inorganic/organic nanofillers into the polymer matrix, such as graphene, C-dots, nano-clay, carbon nanotubes, nano-cellulose, and metal oxides to obtain nanocomposites with excellent dispersion of nanofillers [3–6]. The incorporation of nanofillers into a polymer matrix is usually achieved by either the formation of nanomaterials in-situ on the polymer surface during polymerization, or by bulk polymerization of monomers containing ex-situ preformed nanomaterials in suspension [7,8]. Unlike the ex-situ blending of preformed nanomaterials, in-situ approach allows uniform dispersion of the nanomaterials on the polymer surface, as well as easy control of their size [9]. Besides, polymeric materials have received significant attention because of their ability to control the growth and nucleation of in-situ synthesised nanomaterials as well as to stop the self-aggregation [10,11]. However, despite the various applications and considerable interest of such polymer based nanocomposites, they suffer from poor activity in presence of light. Therefore, to achieve the enhanced optical activity of a photocatalyst, the hybrid composite based photocatalysts and their modification strategies have been explored to increase the photo-generated charge separation in the photocatalyst system. In the semiconductor photocatalysts, the semiconductor can efficiently absorb the broad spectrum of light and generates electron-hole pair, which further migrates to the surface of the photocatalyst, hence readily forms reactive species such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals and degrades the organic pollutants by participating in series of oxidative and reductive reactions [12,13].

Titania nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) are considered as one of the most suitable semiconductor photocatalysts due to their high chemical stability, biocompatibility, low cost and abundant nature [14,15]. However, TiO2 NPs have limited applications in large-scale wastewater treatment for degradation of organic compounds, mainly as a result of photogenerated electron-holes recombination, difficulty in isolating the NPs in aqueous solution, and effectivity in UV light [16,17]. So far many attempts have been made to address these disadvantages of TiO2 NPs as a photocatalyst. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs under visible light has been enhanced by the use of doping metal semiconductors [18], layered semiconductors [19], and quantum dots [20,21], coupling with other semiconductors [22,23], and using surface plasmons of Ag [24] and Au [25] nanoparticles. The toxicity posed because of the use of metal or layered semiconductors, has caused researchers to focus more on the use of quantum dots (QDs). As compared to the conventional metal-based quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs) are superior owing to their high solubility, chemical inertness, easy of modification, and higher resistance to photo-bleaching [26]. In addition, among the other carbon-based materials, carbon dots can easily be synthesised form the carbonaceous building blocks and exhibited excellent optical and diverse applicability. However, their low toxicity, and biocompatibility make them environmentally attractive for different applications. Many synthetic methods have been employed to prepare CDs, including one-pot hydrothermal processes, laser ablation, acidic oxidation, and microwave synthesis, among others [21,27]. The microwave irradiation-based technique has received the utmost attention, as it proceeds rapidly and is comparatively more cost-effective. Furthermore, the photocatalyst fabrication process without using organic solvent, reducing agents, and neutralizing agent is not only a green technique but also highly desirable for wastewater treatment and controlling environmental pollution. In principle, microwaves provide instantaneous energy to a substrate, breaking the chemical bonds of the materials. In so doing, the microwave technique efficiently reduces the reaction time and allows immediate uniform heating, a key aspect to prepare NPs/QDs with uniform size distribution [28–30].

By employing the above considerations, a new hybrid nanocomposite have developed by in-situ formation and deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto crosslinked chitosan [chitosan-grafted poly (vinyl imidazole) [31]] in the presence of diurethane dimethacrylate crosslinker, cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to employ a one-pot synthesis to fabricate functionalized chitosan, followed by in-situ formation of TiO2 NPs and CDs under microwave irradiation at a relatively lower temperature (75 °C). Moreover, an innovative synthesis way for preparation of CDs by in-situ microwave irradiation directly from natural sugar cane juice without any other carbon source is disclosed. The crosslinked chitosan serves as an excellent template for uniform deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs allowing the size of both to be controlled. The composite material demonstrates an admirable efficiency towards degradation of the highly toxic phenolic compound 2,4-dicholorophenol, and the organic dyes such as Reactive Blue 4 (RB4) and Reactive Red 15 (RR15), with an enhanced degradation rate observed under solar light compared to their counter parts.

Chitosan (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), acetic acid (Merck, India), potassium persulphate (KPS, GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Ltd., India), 1-vinylimidazole (VI, Alfa Aesar, United States), diurethane dimethacrylate (DUDMA, Sigma-Aldrich, USA), titanium isopropoxide (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), nitric acid (HNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), acetone (Merck, India) and 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP; TCI, Tokyo, Japan) were used as received. Sugar cane juice was collected from a local market near IIT(ISM), Dhanbad, India. Reactive Blue 4 (RB4) and Reactive Red 15 (RR15) samples were received as gift from Hindustan Gum & Chemicals Ltd. Bhiwani, India.
The microwave-assisted synthesis of the nanocomposite hydrogel [cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs] was achieved as follows.

Chitosan (1 g) was dissolved into 0.2% acetic acid followed by the addition of 0.01 g KPS. VI (6 mL) and DUDMA (1 mL) were added successively under 600 W microwave power (at a temperature of 75 °C for 2 min) to obtain the crosslinked chitosan [31]. Once the gel formed, titanium isopropoxide solution (1.1 mL in 0.01 mL conc. HNO3) was added at one go into the reaction media and then microwave power was increased up to 750 W. The reaction was continued further for 2 min to form the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs nanocomposite hydrogel. A solution of sugar cane juice (9 mL sugar cane juice in 21 mL water and 1 mL of conc. H2SO4, sonicated for 30 min) was added to the reaction mixture and was irradiated for 4 min under microwave irradiation at 750 W (75 °C) (Scheme 1 ). The synthesised cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite hydrogel was precipitated by pouring the mixture into acetone, leaving the homomers, oligomers and unreacted starting materials. The nanocomposite hydrogel was isolated under vacuum filtration, washed with acetone several times and the isolated precipitate was dried at 50 °C under vacuum.
For comparison, the cl-Ch-p(VI) [31], cl-Ch-p(VI)/CDs and cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs were also prepared using microwave irradiation. The detailed methodology is provided in Supporting Information.

The synthesised materials were characterized using solid state UV–Vis spectroscopy, 13C NMR spectroscopy, XPS, XRD, HR-TEM, STEM analyses and surface area measurement. Detailed informations about the instruments and techniques are given in Supporting Information.

The degradation of 2,4-DCP and the organic dyes RB4 and RR15 was performed using the synthesised nanocomposite photocatalyst under sunlight. The rate of degradation was determined using Langmuir−Hinshelwood model (Eq. (1)).
(1)
ln
C 0
/
C t
= kt
where C0 and Ct represent the initial and final concentrations, respectively; k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, and t is the contact time for degradation.

The nanocomposite hydrogel was prepared by free radical polymerization using microwave irradiation under N2 atmosphere. Potassium persulphate (KPS) was used as free radical initiator, which initiates the polymerization process (Scheme 1) [31]. In the chain propagation step, the free radicals on chitosan react with VI to form a graft copolymer radical, which is further crosslinked with DUDMA to form a crosslinked hydrogel [31]. The cationically-functionalized chitosan in strong acidic medium accelerates in-situ formation and stabilization of TiO2 NPs and CDs on the surface of polymer matrix to develop the required cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite hydrogel (Scheme 1). The formation of the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite follows the simultaneous increase of degree of polymerization, in-situ hydrolysis/decomposition of TiO2 and CDs precursors, their growth and stabilization into the polymer matrix.

The in-situ synthesised cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite has shown a green fluorescent colour under irradiation by UV lamp with 365 nm wavelength (Fig. 1a). To investigate the in-situ formation and deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs onto the hydrogel surface, UV–Vis spectral analysis was performed (Fig. 1b). The enhanced UV–Vis absorption of the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite compared to that of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs in the visible region can be ascribed by the presence of CDs on the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs surface. The red shift in the UV–Vis absorption of TiO2 NPs in cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs compared to cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs, indicates the rearrangement of TiO2 NPs energy levels in presence of CDs. This observation manifests the enhanced sunlight utilization efficiency by the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite (Fig. 1b). The UV–Vis spectrum of pure CDs is shown in Fig. S1, Supporting Information. The band gap (3.5 eV, Fig. 1b inset) of TiO2 NPs in cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs is slightly greater than that of anatase TiO2 (3.2 eV). This specifies that TiO2 NPs deposited on the polymer matrix experience high quantum confinement due to their smaller particle size. Moreover, the spectrum of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs also shows an additional weak absorbance maximum at 420 nm, corresponding to CDs [32] on the polymer surface with band gap of 2.52 eV (Fig. 1b inset). The significant lowering of band gap with addition of CDs to the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs composite makes electronic transition more feasible, which in turn allowing the degradation of organic molecules in presence of sunlight with higher efficiency. The in-situ formation of CDs alone on the crosslinked chitosan surface can be inferred by the green fluorescent colour of the material at 365 nm (Fig. S2, Supporting Information).

Formation of cl-Ch-p(VI) and cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite, as well as the interaction of TiO2 NPs and CDs with the hydrogel (as proposed in Scheme 1) were examined by solid state 13C NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). cl-Ch-p(VI) shows characteristic signals between 45.4 ppm and 105.2 ppm (C1-C6) for the chitosan backbone [33] with additional resonances at 23.5 ppm (C7), 136.6 ppm (C8), 130.5 ppm (C9), 157.0 ppm (C10), and 173.2 ppm (C11), indicating the successful polymerization of VI and DUDMA on the chitosan backbone. With incorporation of TiO2 NPs and CDs on the cl-Ch-p(VI) hydrogel surface, these signals have been slightly shifted to downfield, supporting the proposed interaction in between TiO2 NPs and CDs with hydrogel matrix as represented in Scheme 1.

The XRD pattern of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs (Fig. 2a) shows a broad peak at 24.1°, indicating the presence of both carbon with a (002) plane and anatase TiO2 NPs with (101) plane. CDs show low intense peak due to low content and weak diffraction intensity [34]. CDs also show an interlayer d-spacing of 0.208 nm corresponding to (002) plane [34]. The higher diffraction of CDs can be attributed to the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups in the synthesised CDs [35]. The other diffraction peaks at 37.5°, 47.7°, 53.9°, 62.6° and 74.5° arise due to the (004), (200), (105), (204) and (215) crystallographic planes of crystalline TiO2 NPs, respectively [36,37]. The morphology of synthesised cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs was investigated by HRTEM analysis (Fig. 2b). The TEM image enabled the characterization of the size of core CDs, since the organic surface functionalized polymer matrix [cl-Ch-p(VI)] is highly transparent to the electron stream by comparison to the carbon core of CDs. The TEM image shows spherical particles of both the TiO2 NPs and CDs embedded within the polymer matrix. Careful analysis of the TEM image of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs reveals the presence of two types of particles having different contrast: low contrast, comparatively larger particles are crystalline TiO2 NPs with lattice spacing of 0.351 nm (Fig. 2d), while high contrast, smaller particles with lattice spacing of 0.208 nm (Fig. 2c) representing the crystalline CDs. Therefore, the size and the distinct lattice fringes of the two types of particles confirmed the above prediction. The d lattice spacing of TiO2 NPs (0.351 nm) corresponds to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [34].

The STEM image of cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs (Fig. 3 ) suggests that both TiO2 NPs and CDs are well distributed on the crosslinked chitosan surface, highlighting the advantage of using crosslinked chitosan as a suitable template for the proper distribution and uniform growth of TiO2 NPs and CDs.

The pH of the solution plays an important role in the removal of organic dye molecules. The effect of solution pH can be explained by determining point-of-zero charge (PZC) of the catalyst with variation of pH (Fig. S4a, Supporting Information). In the acidic pH (pH < pHpzc), the composite carries a positive charge on the surface due to the protonation of the amino groups as well as pyrrole N atoms. Moreover, RB4 is negatively charged in the investigated pH range. Thus, in a highly acidic environment, the electrostatic attraction (Scheme S1, Supporting Information) between the positively charged composite and the negatively charged dye molecules predominates for adsorption of dye molecules. On the other hand, under basic conditions (pH > pHpzc), excess hydroxyl ions (OH−) adsorb onto the surface of the composite material and dye molecules are unable to interact with composite. Hence % degradation of both the dyes shows maximum degradation at pH 2 (Fig. S4b, Supporting Information).

Hence, the combined effect of TiO2 NPs and CDs in hybrid composite is critical to enhance the rate of dye degradation by the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite. Although, it is difficult to relate, however, the synthesised composite hydrogel has demonstrated comparably better efficacy towards degradation with that of few reported photocatalysts (Table S2, Supporting Information).

Further to investigate the efficacy of the synthesised composite as a degradative photocatalyst, 2,4-DCP degradation was performed using the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite. The nanocomposite material is able to degrade ~95% of highly toxic 2, 4-DCP within 3 h (Fig. 7 ).

The proposed photocatalytic degradation mechanism using the cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs nanocomposite material is illustrated in Scheme 2 . The degradation mechanism can be explained by the following three features.

First, CDs hybridized with TiO2 NPs extend the light response of TiO2 NPs into the visible light region of the solar spectrum by electronic coupling between the π-states of graphite-essential CDs and the band-states of TiO2 NPs [27]. The non-hybrid band of TiO2 NPs are thus narrower than those of pure TiO2 NPs i.e. incorporation of CDs with TiO2 NPs forms a new band energy level, lowering the band gap energy level of pure TiO2 NPs. As a result, a red shift of light, able to excite the electrons to transfer to the conduction band of TiO2 NPs, is reasonable. The nanocomposite can thus use a wide wavelength range of light, from UV to visible range [44,45]. Additionally, the up-conversion properties of CDs may be responsible for transforming the higher wavelength incident light to lower wavelength emitted light [46], allowing a wide range of UV and visible light to be used by the nanocomposite material to degrade the toxic dye molecules. In this aspect, adsorbed molecular oxygen on the surface of the CDs can accept the electrons to form O2 − [27], which is then able to oxidize the dye molecules directly. Second, both the TiO2 NPs and the CDs can jointly form photo-reactive species (O2 − , OH etc.) in presence of sunlight, all of which can easily diffuse to degrade the dye molecules. Finally, CDs can act as electron reservoir to abstract the electrons emitted from TiO2 NPs [27]. When the dye solution is placed in sunlight, the adsorbed dye molecules absorb visible light and are stimulated to the excited state. The excited electrons of the dye molecule are transferred to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 NPs, converting the dye molecules to cationic radical (RB+ ), which are important for photocatalytic degradation. The excited state of the dye molecule is located just above the CB of TiO2 NPs, providing the essential driving force for electron transition from the dye molecule to TiO2 NPs. These electrons in the CB of TiO2 NPs immediately transfer to the CDs, which interact with adsorbed molecular oxygen to form O2 − . The generated O2 − performs a leading role in the degradation of dye molecules in the presence of sunlight. The role of CDs in the degradation process is thus to make the electron transfer easier from the dye to the CB of TiO2 NPs and to increase the efficiency of electron-RB4+ /RR15+ pair separation, enhancing the photodegradation rate (Scheme 2). Moreover, CDs also act as an electron scavenger to minimize the electron-hole pair recombination of TiO2 during the photocatalysis process [47].

To elucidate the degradation mechanism and catalytic fragmentation pathway of respective dyes and 2,4-DCP, LC-MS was performed with degraded dye solution. The intermediate products of RB4 degradation with their respective m/z values are shown in Fig. 8 . From the data, it is clearly evident that the organic dye molecules degrade into small organic/inorganic molecules when using cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs-CDs composite as photocatalyst in presence of sunlight (Fig. 8a). Also, Figs. S6–S8, Supporting Information indicate that the degradation of RB4 was taking place with intermediate m/z values of 317, 283, 240, 164, 149, 148 (proposed degradation pathway is shown in Fig. 8b), and finally into simple inorganic compounds like H2S, HCl, CO2, H2CO3 etc. For RR15, the photodegradation produced fragments with intermediate m/z values of 406, 303, 164, 160, 145, 108, 94 are detected by the LC-MS analysis and shown in Figs. 9a and S9–S13, Supporting Information, degrading into small inorganic/organic molecules such as H2S, CO2, NH4 +, HCl, H2SO4, H2CO3, CH3-NH2, NH2-CH2-NH2 etc. (proposed degradation pathway is shown in Fig. 9b). Also, LC-MS study was performed to determine the degradation pathway of 2,4-DCP, by identifying the intermediate products at various time intervals, with the degraded products being confirmed by their respective m/z values in Figs. 9a and S14, Supporting Information. In the catalytic fragmentation mechanism (Fig. 9b), the electron holes and OH radicals play an important role to eliminate Cl atoms and cleave the aromatic ring of 2,4-DCP into small molecules. Thus LC-MS study specifies the successful degradation of organic molecules into smaller molecules (Fig. 10 ).

From the above observations and discussions, it is thus obvious that a new biopolymeric nanocomposite hydrogel was successfully developed under microwave irradiation through in-situ formation and deposition of TiO2 NPs and CDs on the surface of copolymer matrix. The single pot in-situ fabrication process was found to be worthy towards simultaneous increase of degree of polymerization, hydrolysis/decomposition of the TiO2 and CDs precursors and their growth into the polymer matrix under solvent free condition at comparatively low temperature (75 °C). The hybrid nanocomposite material demonstrated excellent degradative ability, being able to decompose toxic organic molecules through photocatalytic degradation on exposure to sunlight, which showed excellent performance compared to only titania based (i.e. cl-Ch-p(VI)/TiO2NPs) and CDs based (cl-Ch-p(VI)/CDs) composite materials. Therefore, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the hybrid composite can be explained by the tuned band gap of the semiconductors and synergistic effect of TiO2 NPs and CDs. In addition, this facile synthetic strategy can be used for synthesis of other CDs-based hybrid polymeric nanocomposite materials for photocatalytic degradation of other toxic organic compounds to simpler molecules.
Lipi Midya: Investigation and Methodology.
Abanindra Nath Sarkar: Investigation and Methodology.
Raghunath Das: Validation, Formal analysis.
Arjun Maity: Supervision.
Sagar Pal: Conceptualization and Supervision.

Authors thankfully acknowledge the financial support from Department of Science & Technology, New Delhi, India [Ref. No: DST/TM/WTI/2 K16/42 (G)] and Council of Scientific & Industrial Research, New Delhi [Scheme No: 01(2892)/17/EMR-II]. Authors earnestly acknowledge SAIF, IISc, Bangalore for providing solid state 13C NMR data and CRF, IIT Kharagpur for LCMS data.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.08.230.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. C. Colmenares, E. Kuna, Photoactive hybrid catalysts based on natural and synthetic polymers: A comparative overview, Molecules 22 (2017), 790.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong and conductive reduced graphene oxide/polyester resin composite films with improved mechanical strength, thermal stability and its antibacterial activity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of highly exfoliated polyester clay nanocomposites: process-property correlations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Dalir, R. D. Farahani, V. Nhim, B. Samson, M. L. Levesque, D. Therriault, Preparation of highly exfoliated polyester clay nanocomposites: process-property correlations, Langmuir 28 (2012) 791−803.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dispersion and rheology of multiwalled carbon nanotubes in unsaturated polyester resin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. E. U Ureña-Benavides, M. J. Kayatin, V. A. Davis, Dispersion and rheology of multiwalled carbon nanotubes in unsaturated polyester resin, Macromolecules 46 (2013) 1642−1650.
